text: Nike Vaporfly 4% shoes reduce oxygen consumption by 4.3-4.4% compared to habitual running shoes across submaximal intensities in male recreational runners.
Lightweight racing flats (Saucony Endorphin) reduce oxygen consumption by 2.7-3.4% compared to habitual running shoes, though this difference did not reach statistical significance.
The running economy difference between Nike Vaporfly 4% and lightweight racing flats is small (1.0-1.7%) and not statistically significant.
Nike Vaporfly 4% improved 3-km treadmill time trial performance by 16.6 seconds (2.4%) compared to habitual shoes and by 13.0 seconds (1.8%) compared to lightweight racing flats.
specific_value: 16.6s / 2.4% vs own shoes; 13.0s / 1.8% vs racing flats

text: Individual responses to super shoes vary widely, with running economy changes ranging from -10.3% to +13.3% and time trial improvements from -4.7% to +9.3%, indicating shoe selection should be individualized.
specific_value: RE range: -10.3% to +13.3%; TT range: -4.7% to +9.3%

text: 61% of recreational runners ran their fastest 3-km time trial in Nike Vaporfly 4%, compared to 22% in lightweight racing flats and 17% in their own shoes.
specific_value: 61% fastest in Vaporfly, 22% in racing flats, 17% in own shoes
